skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy (CON-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Constitutional Principles for AP US Government & Politics students (AP Gov Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy, CON-1). Students should understand the foundational principles embedded in the U.S. Constitution. Key concepts: popular sovereignty (government derives authority from the people); limited government (government power is restricted by law); separation of powers (legislative, executive, judicial branches have distinct roles); checks and balances (each branch can limit the others); federalism (power divided between national and state governments); republican government (representatives act on behalf of the people). Students should understand the debates between Federalists and Anti-Federalists, how the Constitution balances majority rule with minority rights, and how these principles shape contemporary governance. Required foundational documents: the Constitution, Federalist No. 10 (factions), Federalist No. 51 (separation of powers), Brutus No. 1 (Anti-Federalist critique). Vocabulary: popular sovereignty, limited government, separation of powers, checks and balances, federalism, republic, ratification, enumerated powers.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy (LOR-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Federalism for AP US Government & Politics students (AP Gov Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy, LOR-1). Students should understand how power is divided and shared between national and state governments. Key concepts: enumerated powers (specifically granted to the federal government, e.g., declare war, regulate interstate commerce); reserved powers (retained by states under the 10th Amendment, e.g., education, police powers); concurrent powers (shared, e.g., taxation); the Supremacy Clause (federal law overrides conflicting state law); the evolution of federalism from dual (layer cake) to cooperative (marble cake) to new federalism (devolution); fiscal federalism (categorical grants, block grants, unfunded mandates). Key cases: McCulloch v. Maryland (implied powers, national supremacy), United States v. Lopez (limits on commerce clause). Students should analyze how federalism creates policy diversity and debate. Vocabulary: federalism, enumerated powers, reserved powers, concurrent powers, Supremacy Clause, implied powers, devolution, mandate.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs (MPA-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Political Beliefs and Behaviors for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs, MPA-1). Students should understand how Americans form political opinions and how those beliefs influence political participation. Key concepts: political socialization (family, peers, media, education, religion shape political views); public opinion polls (sampling, margin of error, random sampling, question wording effects); ideological spectrums (liberal/conservative, libertarian/authoritarian); how demographics (race, gender, age, income, education, religion, geography) correlate with political beliefs; the role of media in shaping public opinion; the difference between political culture (shared values) and political ideology (coherent belief systems). Students should interpret polling data, identify factors that influence political attitudes, and understand how public opinion both shapes and is shaped by government action. Vocabulary: political socialization, public opinion, ideology, liberal, conservative, polling, sampling, margin of error, demographic.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation (PMI-2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Political Parties and Interest Groups for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation, PMI-2). Students should understand how political parties and interest groups link citizens to government. Key concepts: the two-party system and why third parties struggle (winner-take-all elections, ballot access laws); party functions (nominating candidates, mobilizing voters, organizing government); party coalitions and realignment; interest groups influence policy through lobbying, campaign contributions (PACs and Super PACs), grassroots mobilization, litigation, and public education; iron triangles and issue networks; the difference between parties (seek to win elections) and interest groups (seek to influence policy without running candidates). Students should analyze how these organizations both enhance and sometimes distort democratic participation. Required cases: Citizens United v. FEC (corporate political speech). Vocabulary: two-party system, coalition, realignment, interest group, lobbying, PAC, Super PAC, iron triangle, grassroots, pluralism.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation (PRD-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Elections and Voting Behavior for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation, PRD-1). Students should understand how elections work in the United States and what influences voter behavior. Key concepts: the electoral process (primaries, caucuses, national conventions, general election); the Electoral College (winner-take-all in most states, 270 to win, small-state advantage); factors influencing voter turnout (registration requirements, voter ID laws, convenience of voting, political efficacy); models of voting behavior (party identification, candidate characteristics, issues/policy positions); the impact of campaign finance (fundraising, spending, advertising); gerrymandering and its effects on representation; midterm vs. presidential election dynamics. Students should analyze election data, evaluate the effects of electoral rules on outcomes, and understand debates about electoral reform. Vocabulary: Electoral College, primary, caucus, gerrymandering, voter turnout, political efficacy, campaign finance, swing state, incumbent.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (CON-2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Legislative Branch for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, CON-2). Students should understand the structure, powers, and processes of Congress. Key concepts: bicameralism (House vs. Senate differences in size, terms, constituencies, and powers); the legislative process (committee system, hearings, markup, floor debate, conference committees, presidential action); congressional powers (taxing, spending, commerce clause, war powers, oversight, impeachment); the role of party leadership (Speaker, Majority Leader, whips); how members balance representational roles (trustee vs. delegate vs. politico models); the filibuster and cloture in the Senate; logrolling and pork-barrel legislation. Students should analyze how institutional structure affects policy outcomes and how Congress interacts with other branches. Required documents: Constitution Article I. Vocabulary: bicameralism, committee, filibuster, cloture, markup, appropriations, oversight, impeachment, logrolling, gerrymandering, reapportionment.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (CON-3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Executive Branch for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, CON-3). Students should understand the powers, roles, and limitations of the presidency. Key concepts: formal (constitutional) powers include commander-in-chief, veto, appointment, treaty-making (with Senate advice and consent), and pardons; informal powers include executive orders, executive agreements, signing statements, persuasion (bully pulpit), and agenda-setting; the expansion of presidential power over time; the cabinet and Executive Office of the President (including the National Security Council and Office of Management and Budget); checks on presidential power (congressional override, impeachment, judicial review, public opinion). Students should analyze the tension between presidential leadership and constitutional constraints. Required documents: Constitution Article II, Federalist No. 70 (energy in the executive). Vocabulary: executive order, veto, bully pulpit, cabinet, commander-in-chief, appointment power, executive privilege, signing statement.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (CON-4)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Judicial Branch for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, CON-4). Students should understand the role, structure, and power of the federal judiciary, especially the Supreme Court. Key concepts: judicial review (the power to declare laws unconstitutional, established in Marbury v. Madison); the appointment process (presidential nomination, Senate confirmation, lifetime tenure); the structure of the federal court system (district courts, courts of appeals, Supreme Court); how cases reach the Supreme Court (writ of certiorari, Rule of Four); judicial philosophies (judicial activism vs. judicial restraint, originalism vs. living constitution); the impact of Supreme Court decisions on public policy and individual rights. Students should analyze how the judiciary balances legal interpretation with political implications. Required cases: Marbury v. Madison. Required documents: Constitution Article III, Federalist No. 78 (judicial independence). Vocabulary: judicial review, precedent, writ of certiorari, activism, restraint, originalism, dissent, majority opinion, stare decisis.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights (LOR-2, LOR-3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Civil Liberties and Civil Rights for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights, LOR-2, LOR-3). Students should understand the constitutional protections for individual freedoms and the struggle for equal treatment under law. Key concepts: civil liberties (protections against government action, primarily in the Bill of Rights): First Amendment freedoms (speech, religion, press, assembly, petition), due process rights (4th, 5th, 6th, 8th Amendments), and the incorporation doctrine (applying the Bill of Rights to states via the 14th Amendment); civil rights (protections against discrimination): the Equal Protection Clause, strict scrutiny vs. rational basis review, landmark legislation (Civil Rights Act of 1964, Voting Rights Act of 1965). Required cases: Engel v. Vitale, Tinker v. Des Moines, Schenck v. US, Gideon v. Wainwright, McDonald v. Chicago, Brown v. Board. Students should analyze tensions between competing rights and between liberty and order. Vocabulary: civil liberties, civil rights, incorporation, due process, equal protection, strict scrutiny, establishment clause, free exercise.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (PMI-3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Public Policy for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, PMI-3). Students should understand how public policy is created, implemented, and evaluated in the American political system. Key concepts: the policy-making process (problem identification, agenda setting, policy formulation, adoption, implementation, evaluation); types of policy (regulatory, distributive, redistributive); the role of each branch in policymaking (Congress legislates, the president signs/vetoes and directs agencies, the judiciary reviews constitutionality); the influence of interest groups, media, and public opinion on policy; policy implementation challenges (bureaucratic discretion, funding, compliance); how federalism creates policy variation across states. Students should analyze specific policy examples and trace how political actors interact throughout the policy process. Connect to current events and ongoing policy debates. Vocabulary: policy agenda, regulatory policy, distributive policy, redistributive policy, implementation, evaluation, discretion, mandate.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation (MPA-2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Media and Politics for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation, MPA-2). Students should understand the role of media in American democracy and how it shapes political behavior. Key concepts: traditional media (newspapers, television, radio) vs. new media (social media, online news, blogs, podcasts); the media as a watchdog (investigative journalism, holding government accountable); agenda-setting (media determines which issues receive public attention); framing (how issues are presented influences public perception); media bias (partisan outlets, confirmation bias among consumers); the evolution from gatekeeping (editors deciding what's newsworthy) to the current environment where anyone can publish content; the impact of social media on political discourse (echo chambers, misinformation, rapid mobilization). Students should analyze how media consumption patterns affect political knowledge and participation. Vocabulary: agenda-setting, framing, watchdog, gatekeeping, media bias, echo chamber, misinformation, new media, press freedom.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation (PRD-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Political Participation for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation, PRD-1). Students should understand the various ways citizens engage in the political process beyond voting. Key concepts: forms of participation (voting, contacting officials, campaigning, protesting, running for office, joining interest groups, community organizing, social media activism); factors affecting participation levels (socioeconomic status, education, age, race, political efficacy, mobilization efforts); the role of social movements in driving political change; barriers to participation (voter registration requirements, felony disenfranchisement, political apathy); the debate over whether increased participation always strengthens democracy. Students should analyze trends in political participation, evaluate the effectiveness of different forms of engagement, and understand how the expansion of participatory opportunities through technology has changed American politics. Vocabulary: political participation, voter turnout, political efficacy, social movement, civic engagement, protest, mobilization, disenfranchisement.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy (LOR-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers State and Local Government for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy, LOR-1). Students should understand how state and local governments function within the federal system. Key concepts: state governments mirror the federal structure (governor, legislature, judiciary) but vary in organization; states serve as 'laboratories of democracy' where policy innovations are tested; state constitutions can provide broader rights than the U.S. Constitution; local governments (counties, cities, school districts, special districts) derive authority from states (Dillon's Rule vs. home rule); the role of state governments in education, criminal justice, infrastructure, and public health; intergovernmental relations (cooperative federalism, mandates, grants); variation in state policies (taxes, gun laws, marijuana legalization) reflecting regional differences. Students should analyze how federalism creates both policy diversity and inequality. Vocabulary: laboratories of democracy, home rule, Dillon's Rule, state constitution, governor, state legislature, local government, intergovernmental.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation (PRD-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Campaign Finance for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation, PRD-1). Students should understand the rules, controversies, and impacts of money in American elections. Key concepts: the Federal Election Commission (FEC) regulates campaign finance; key legislation includes the Federal Election Campaign Act (FECA), Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA/McCain-Feingold); contribution limits apply to individuals and PACs; Super PACs can raise unlimited funds but cannot coordinate with candidates (Citizens United v. FEC, 2010); 501(c)(4) organizations (dark money groups) can engage in political activity without disclosing donors; the debate between free speech (money as political expression) and preventing corruption (unequal influence). Students should analyze how campaign finance rules affect electoral competition, the role of wealthy donors and corporations, and ongoing debates about reform. Vocabulary: campaign finance, PAC, Super PAC, dark money, FEC, contribution limit, Citizens United, disclosure, soft money, hard money.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs (MPA-1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Political Ideologies for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs, MPA-1). Students should understand the major political ideologies that shape American political debate. Key concepts: liberalism (supports government intervention in the economy, social safety nets, environmental regulation, and progressive taxation while emphasizing individual civil liberties and social equality); conservatism (favors limited government, free markets, traditional social values, strong national defense, and individual responsibility); libertarianism (minimal government involvement in both economic and social affairs); populism (champions the common people against elites); the left-right political spectrum and its limitations; how ideological positions map onto specific policy debates (healthcare, immigration, gun control, climate change, taxation). Students should identify ideological positions in political statements, understand that most Americans hold a mix of views, and analyze how ideology shapes party platforms. Vocabulary: liberalism, conservatism, libertarianism, populism, ideology, political spectrum, left, right, moderate, progressive.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (PMI-2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Bureaucracy and Public Administration for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, PMI-2). Students should understand how the federal bureaucracy implements policy and the challenges of administrative governance. Key concepts: the bureaucracy consists of cabinet departments, independent agencies, regulatory commissions, and government corporations; bureaucratic functions include implementation, regulation, and administration; bureaucratic discretion allows agencies to interpret and apply laws; the merit system (civil service) replaced the spoils system; iron triangles (agency-committee-interest group alliances) and issue networks influence policy; mechanisms of oversight include congressional hearings, the Government Accountability Office, and presidential appointments; challenges include red tape, inefficiency, and accountability concerns. Students should analyze how unelected bureaucrats exercise significant policy influence and evaluate methods of accountability. Vocabulary: bureaucracy, discretion, implementation, regulation, merit system, iron triangle, issue network, red tape, oversight, civil service.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (CON-5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Budget Process for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, CON-5). Students should understand how the federal government raises and spends money and the political dynamics of budgeting. Key concepts: the president submits a budget proposal to Congress; the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) provides independent analysis; Congress passes appropriations bills through committees; mandatory spending (entitlements like Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid) vs. discretionary spending (defense, education, research); revenue sources (income tax, payroll tax, corporate tax, borrowing); the national debt and annual deficits; continuing resolutions and government shutdowns when budgets are not passed; the debt ceiling debate; the politics of fiscal policy (tax cuts vs. spending increases, austerity vs. stimulus). Students should analyze how budget priorities reflect political values and the challenges of balancing competing demands. Vocabulary: budget, appropriations, mandatory spending, discretionary spending, deficit, debt, entitlement, CBO, revenue, fiscal policy, debt ceiling.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (CON-3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Foreign Policy and National Security for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, CON-3). Students should understand how foreign policy is made and the roles of different actors in national security decisions. Key concepts: the president is commander-in-chief and chief diplomat (executive agreements, diplomatic recognition); Congress declares war, controls military funding, and ratifies treaties; the War Powers Resolution attempts to limit unilateral presidential military action; key agencies include the State Department, Defense Department, CIA, and NSC; foreign policy approaches include diplomacy, economic sanctions, military intervention, and multilateral cooperation; the tension between presidential war powers and congressional authority; the role of international organizations (UN, NATO, WTO). Students should analyze how domestic politics influence foreign policy and evaluate the effectiveness of different foreign policy tools. Vocabulary: commander-in-chief, War Powers Resolution, diplomacy, sanctions, treaty, executive agreement, national security, NSC, multilateral.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches (PMI-3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Policy Analysis for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches, PMI-3). Students should understand how to systematically analyze public policy proposals and outcomes. Key concepts: identifying the problem a policy aims to address; evaluating policy alternatives based on effectiveness, efficiency, equity, and feasibility; understanding trade-offs (every policy choice has costs and benefits); the role of evidence and data in policy evaluation; the difference between intended and unintended consequences; cost-benefit analysis; how different stakeholders (government, businesses, citizens, interest groups) are affected by policy choices; the importance of implementation in determining policy success. Students should analyze real policy examples, evaluate competing proposals, and construct evidence-based arguments about policy effectiveness. This skill is essential for the AP exam's free-response questions requiring argumentation. Vocabulary: policy analysis, trade-off, cost-benefit, stakeholder, effectiveness, efficiency, equity, unintended consequence, evidence-based, implementation.

skills.ref: AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation (MPA-3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Contemporary Political Issues for AP Gov students (AP Gov Unit 5: Political Participation, MPA-3). Students should understand how current political debates connect to enduring constitutional principles and political processes. Key concepts: contemporary issues reflect ongoing tensions in American democracy (individual rights vs. public safety, federal power vs. state authority, equality vs. liberty); students should apply their understanding of government institutions, political processes, and constitutional principles to analyze current events; examples include debates over healthcare policy, immigration reform, gun control, climate change, technology regulation, voting rights, and Supreme Court decisions. Students should identify the constitutional and institutional dimensions of current issues, understand multiple perspectives, and construct evidence-based arguments. This topic integrates all course themes and prepares students for the argument essay on the AP exam. Vocabulary: contemporary issues, political debate, constitutional principles, public policy, partisanship, compromise, stakeholder, evidence-based argument.
